blurb: Colorado's most common native lupine — silvery leaves, blue-violet spires.
seasons: Blue-violet spikes Jun–Aug; dies fully to ground in winter
wildlife: Xerces bee value; butterfly host; nitrogen-fixer
toxic: All parts (esp. seeds) toxic if eaten — caution with kids/pets/livestock

care.hardiness: Hardy to USDA Zone 4 (and likely colder) — fully winter-hardy across the Front Range (Zone 5b–6a). The foliage dies completely to the ground after frost and re-emerges from the root crown in spring, which is normal. No winter protection needed.
care.sun: Full sun is best, though it tolerates light afternoon shade. In its native Rocky Mountain habitat it grows from open sagebrush foothills to subalpine meadows at 6,500–10,000 ft — the intense Front Range sun suits it perfectly. Plants in too much shade become leggy and flower poorly.
care.soil: Well-drained soils of almost any texture — sandy loam, loam, gravel, even lean clay — as long as it doesn't sit wet. It tolerates a wide pH range (roughly 5.7–7.5), so the alkaline clay-loam of Front Range gardens is acceptable, though adding coarse grit improves drainage in heavier soils. As a nitrogen-fixing legume it thrives in poor, unamended soil and needs no rich compost.
care.water: Low water once established — it is adapted to as little as 10–18 in. of annual precipitation. Water transplants and first-year plants regularly to help them root in; after that, monthly deep watering through summer drought is usually sufficient. Do not overwater; wet soil encourages crown rot.
care.spacing: Space plants 12–18 in. apart. Mature clumps reach about 1–2 ft wide and 1–2 ft tall. It colonizes gradually by seed but is not aggressive; leave a few seed pods to self-sow for a naturalized colony effect.
care.sow: Direct-sow in fall for natural cold stratification over winter, or start in early spring after stratifying seed. Sow where plants are to grow — lupines develop a deep taproot and resent transplanting once established. Scratch seed shallowly into firm, weed-free soil and tamp well. Soaking seed in hot water (not boiling) for 12–24 hours, or nicking the hard seed coat with sandpaper (scarification), greatly improves germination.
care.stratify: Cold-moist stratification for 30 days improves germination of stored seed. Mix seed with damp sand in a sealed bag and refrigerate for 4 weeks before spring sowing. Fall-sown seed stratifies naturally over winter outdoors. Scarification (lightly abrading the seed coat) can substitute for or complement cold treatment — use whichever is convenient.
care.depth: Sow ½–¾ in. deep and firm the soil. Germination typically takes 2–4 weeks once soil warms to 55–65°F in spring. Expect uneven emergence; the taproot develops before much top growth appears.
care.bloom: Blue-violet (occasionally white or pink) pea-flower spikes from late May to mid-August on the Front Range — peak bloom is typically June and July. First-year plants from seed usually bloom lightly; full flowering develops in year two.
care.feeding: As a native nitrogen-fixer it feeds itself through its root-bacteria partnership and needs no added fertilizer. Avoid nitrogen-rich amendments, which push leafy growth at the expense of flowers.
care.maintenance: Cut spent flower spikes back to basal foliage after bloom if you want to tidy the plant; leave some to set seed for self-sowing and wildlife forage. The foliage yellows and dies back fully in fall — cut it to the ground after frost. Plants live 3–5 years and are best renewed by allowing self-seeding rather than division (the taproot dislikes disturbance).
care.selfsow: Colonizes by seed — seed pods ripen and split explosively in late summer. Allow a few pods to mature for self-sowing naturalization. Volunteers pull easily when small if you need to edit the colony.
care.troubles: Generally robust on the Front Range. Slugs can nip new spring growth in moist years; aphids occasionally cluster on stems. All parts — especially seeds and pods — contain toxic alkaloids (quinolizidine alkaloids) that can cause serious illness in livestock and humans if ingested in quantity; keep children and pets from eating seed pods. Handle seed and cut stems with care. No serious diseases in well-drained, sunny sites.
care.harvest: Let pods ripen to tan/brown and collect just before they split (they eject seed forcibly). Dry pods in a paper bag, shake free the seeds, and store cool and dry. Germination is best with scarification or cold stratification.
care.companions: Native grasses (little bluestem, blue grama), Rocky Mountain penstemon, blanketflower, aspen fleabane, and prairie zinnia — dry-meadow and open-slope plants that share its low-water, well-drained requirements.
